title: American Pot Roast
subtitle: Beef chuck braised in red wine and stock until fork-tender, with root vegetables
description: American pot roast is Sunday dinner — a whole piece of beef chuck braised low and slow in the oven in red wine and beef stock with onions, carrots, and potatoes until completely tender and the braising liquid has reduced to a glossy, deeply savoury sauce. Unlike a cut-up stew, the whole roast cooks in one piece and is carved or pulled at the table. It is one of the defining dishes of American home cooking.
sourceNotes: Pot roast is a foundational dish of American home cooking, appearing in Fannie Farmer's The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896) and a fixture of American cookbooks throughout the twentieth century. The method of braising a whole cut in the oven is adapted from European braise traditions brought by German, Eastern European, and British immigrants.

recipeTools.notes: Large enough to hold the beef roast with room around it for the vegetables. Oven-safe with a tight-fitting lid.
Target 95 °C for pot-roast consistency — well beyond food-safe minimum, but the temperature at which chuck becomes pull-tender.

glossaryTerms.definition: A moist-heat method: food is browned, then cooked slowly in a covered vessel with a small amount of liquid. The long cook tenderises the collagen in heavily worked cuts and produces a rich, reduced sauce.

ingredients.notes: A single boneless chuck roast, tied with kitchen string if necessary to hold its shape
Cut into large pieces — added after 2 hours

steps: Heat the oven to 150 °C fan (170 °C / gas 3). Season the beef all over with salt and pepper and dust with flour.
Heat the oil in a large casserole over a high heat. Sear the beef on all four sides for 4–5 minutes per side until deeply browned. Remove to a plate.
Reduce to medium heat. Cook the onion wedges in the remaining fat for 4–5 minutes until beginning to colour. Add the garlic and tomato purée and cook for 1 minute.

Pour in the red wine and let it bubble for 2 minutes, scraping up all the fond. Add the hot beef stock and Worcestershire sauce. Return the beef to the pot — the liquid should come about halfway up the meat.
Add the thyme and bay leaves. Bring to a simmer, cover tightly, and transfer to the oven.

Braise for 2 hours. Remove the lid and add the carrots and potatoes around the beef. Cover and return to the oven for a further 45–60 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the beef is completely soft — it should yield easily to a fork or light pressure.
Rest the beef in the casserole, covered, for 20 minutes before serving.
Remove the herbs. If the braising liquid is thin, transfer it to a saucepan and reduce over a high heat for 5–10 minutes until slightly thickened and glossy.
Carve or pull the beef at the table. Serve with the vegetables and the reduced braising liquid as the sauce.

content.text: The sear on the beef before

is the flavour base of everything that follows. Sear all four flat sides (bottom, top, and both long sides) until deeply, confidently brown. This takes 16–20 minutes total and requires patience and high heat. Pale searing produces a pale sauce.

Heat the oven to 150 °C fan. Season {{beef-chuck}} of beef and dust with flour. Sear all sides in hot oil for 4–5 minutes per side. Remove. Cook onion wedges in the remaining fat for 4–5 minutes, add garlic and tomato purée for 1 minute. Add {{red-wine}} of red wine and scrape the base, then add {{beef-stock}} of hot stock and Worcestershire sauce. Return the beef, add herbs, cover, and braise for 2 hours.

Add {{carrot}} of carrots and {{waxy-potatoes}} of halved potatoes. Return to the oven covered for 45–60 minutes until the beef is fork-tender. Rest covered for 20 minutes. If the sauce is thin, reduce it in a separate pan. Carve or pull the beef and serve with the vegetables and reduced braising liquid, scattered with parsley.
